openmeter/secret/adapter.purpose: Plaintext (no-op) implementation of secret.Adapter — stores secrets as their raw value in the SecretID itself. This is a stub implementation; a production deployment would replace it with a real secret store (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.).

patterns.name: Interface compliance assertion
patterns.description: Every adapter file must declare `var _ secret.Adapter = (*adapter)(nil)` at the top to catch interface drift at compile time.

patterns.name: Stateless adapter struct
patterns.description: The adapter struct holds no fields because secrets are encoded into SecretID. If a real backend is added, inject the client via New() and store it on the struct.

patterns.name: No Ent dependency in this implementation
patterns.description: Despite the component description mentioning Ent, this adapter uses only secretentity types — no *entdb.Client. A future real adapter would receive a client via New().

key_file_guides.role: Constructor and compile-time interface assertion. Wire calls New() to obtain a secret.Adapter.
key_file_guides.watch_for: If secret.Adapter gains new methods, the compile-time assertion will fail here first — add the method to secret.go.

key_file_guides.role: All four CRUD methods: CreateAppSecret, UpdateAppSecret, GetAppSecret, DeleteAppSecret. All are no-ops that encode the value into the SecretID.
key_file_guides.watch_for: CreateAppSecret and UpdateAppSecret return secretentity.NewSecretID(input.AppID, input.Value, input.Key) — the ID IS the plaintext value. A real implementation must never do this.

anti_patterns: Storing state on the adapter struct without injecting a client through New()
Returning errors from DeleteAppSecret or CreateAppSecret in the stub — callers expect these to always succeed
Importing openmeter/ent/db in this package — this stub has no DB dependency; Ent belongs in a future real adapter sub-package
Adding business logic here — validation belongs in secretservice, not the adapter

decisions.decision: SecretID encodes the plaintext value as the ID field
decisions.rationale: Simplest possible stub that satisfies the Adapter interface without an external secret store; callers reconstruct the value from GetAppSecret by reading input.ID directly.

openmeter/secret/entity.purpose: Pure domain types for the secret package: SecretID (namespaced composite key), Secret (ID + value), all input structs, and the SecretNotFoundError sentinel. No persistence logic lives here.

patterns.name: Validate() on every input type
patterns.description: All input structs (CreateAppSecretInput, UpdateAppSecretInput) expose a Validate() error method that wraps field errors in models.NewGenericValidationError. Service layer calls Validate() before delegating to the adapter.
patterns.example: func (i CreateAppSecretInput) Validate() error { if i.Key == "" { return models.NewGenericValidationError(errors.New("key is required")) }; return nil }

patterns.name: Embed models.NamespacedID in identity types
patterns.description: SecretID embeds models.NamespacedID so it carries both Namespace and ID and can reuse NamespacedID.Validate().
patterns.example: type SecretID struct { models.NamespacedID; AppID app.AppID; Key string }

patterns.name: Error wraps models.GenericNotFoundError
patterns.description: SecretNotFoundError wraps models.NewGenericNotFoundError so the HTTP encoder maps it to 404 automatically. Add `var _ models.GenericError = (*SecretNotFoundError)(nil)` for compile-time verification.

patterns.name: Type aliases for symmetric input types
patterns.description: GetAppSecretInput and DeleteAppSecretInput are type aliases for SecretID, avoiding redundant wrapper structs when the input is just an identifier.

key_file_guides.role: SecretID and Secret domain types plus NewSecretID constructor and Validate methods.
key_file_guides.watch_for: SecretID.Validate() chains NamespacedID.Validate() → AppID.Validate() → Key check; all must pass. Skipping AppID.Validate() lets invalid IDs reach the adapter.

key_file_guides.role: All four input structs. GetAppSecretInput and DeleteAppSecretInput are aliases, not new types.
key_file_guides.watch_for: UpdateAppSecretInput has both AppID and SecretID — Validate() calls both. New fields must be validated here, not in the service.
Always use models.NewGenericNotFoundError as the inner error so the HTTP layer maps it to 404 without special-casing.

anti_patterns: Adding persistence logic or Ent imports to this package — it must remain a pure domain type package
Returning raw errors instead of models.NewGenericValidationError from Validate() — breaks HTTP status code mapping
Creating new input structs without a Validate() method — service layer calls Validate() unconditionally
Duplicating validation logic in the adapter that already exists in input Validate() methods

decisions.decision: Input types live in a separate entity sub-package rather than in the root secret package
decisions.rationale: Breaks the import cycle: input.go imports openmeter/app for AppID, while openmeter/app imports openmeter/secret — placing types in a sub-package (secretentity) lets both import it without a cycle.

openmeter/secret/service.purpose: Concrete implementation of secret.Service that validates inputs then delegates all persistence to the injected secret.Adapter. Contains no business logic beyond validation.

patterns.name: Config struct constructor with Validate()
patterns.description: New(config Config) (*Service, error) validates config before constructing. Config.Validate() checks that required fields (Adapter) are non-nil. This is the standard service constructor pattern in this repo.
patterns.example: func New(config Config) (*Service, error) { if err := config.Validate(); err != nil { return nil, err }; return &Service{adapter: config.Adapter}, nil }

patterns.name: Double interface assertion
patterns.description: service.go asserts `var _ secret.Service = (*Service)(nil)` and secret.go asserts `var _ secret.SecretService = (*Service)(nil)` — both must be kept in sync when secret.Service changes.

patterns.name: Validate-then-delegate in every method
patterns.description: Each service method calls input.Validate() and wraps any error in models.NewGenericValidationError before calling the adapter. No logic beyond validation.
patterns.example: func (s *Service) CreateAppSecret(ctx context.Context, input secretentity.CreateAppSecretInput) (secretentity.SecretID, error) { if err := input.Validate(); err != nil { return secretentity.SecretID{}, models.NewGenericValidationError(fmt.Errorf("error create app secret: %w", err)) }; return s.adapter.CreateAppSecret(ctx, input) }

key_file_guides.role: Service struct, Config struct, New() constructor. Only field is adapter secret.Adapter.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Config.Validate() only checks Adapter != nil. If new required dependencies are added to Service, add them to Config and Validate() here.

key_file_guides.role: All four method implementations (Create, Update, Get, Delete). Each wraps adapter call with validation.
key_file_guides.watch_for: UpdateAppSecret returns input.SecretID (not zero value) on validation error — intentional to return the original ID. Other methods return zero values on error.

anti_patterns: Adding business logic or cross-domain calls to this service — it is a thin validation+delegation layer only
Calling the adapter without first calling input.Validate() — adapter is a stub and won't catch bad inputs
Injecting *entdb.Client directly into Service — persistence is the adapter's concern; Service only holds secret.Adapter
Bypassing the Config struct and constructing Service{} directly — Validate() in New() is the only nil-safety gate

decisions.decision: Service is a thin pass-through with validation only
decisions.rationale: The secret domain has no business rules beyond ensuring inputs are structurally valid before reaching the adapter; all complexity lives in the adapter implementation (real or stub).
